disease.name: Hiatal Hernia
disease.note: Herniation of abdominal contents through the esophageal hiatus into the chest. Sliding (Type I) is most common and causes GERD. Paraesophageal (Types II-IV) contains stomach in the chest and carries risk of gastric volvulus and strangulation. In East Africa, obesity is rising and so is GERD, but paraesophageal hernias remain less common.
presentingComplaints: Heartburn rising up to the throat
Food or sour liquid coming back up
Feeling full or bloated after small meals
pathophysiologyDetail: Hiatal hernia anatomy: esophageal hiatus is formed by the right and left crura of the diaphragm (muscular sling); weakening of the phrenoesophageal ligament — a fibroelastic membrane anchoring the GEJ to the hiatus — allows upward herniation

Type I (sliding): GEJ and proximal stomach slide above the diaphragm through the hiatus — the most common type (85-90%); the herniated portion is covered by peritoneum; the hernia is reducible and moves with swallowing and position changes

Type II (rolling/paraesophageal): GEJ remains in normal position; gastric fundus herniates through the hiatus alongside the esophagus into the posterior mediastinum — a true paraesophageal hernia with a peritoneal sac
Type III (mixed): both GEJ and fundus herniate above the diaphragm — features of both sliding and paraesophageal; the GEJ is above the diaphragm and the fundus is herniated alongside
Type IV (giant): hernia sac contains stomach PLUS other organs (colon, omentum, spleen, small bowel); the most severe form; the stomach may be entirely in the chest with organoaxial rotation

Phrenoesophageal ligament degeneration: age-related collagen weakening (type I to III collagen ratio change) and chronic increased intra-abdominal pressure stretch the ligament → widening of hiatus → herniation through the defect

Anti-reflux barrier disruption: diaphragmatic crura normally augment the lower esophageal sphincter (LES); when the GEJ migrates above the diaphragm, the crural pinch is lost → LES pressure drops from ~20 mmHg to ~10 mmHg → acid reflux

Cameron ulcers: linear erosions at the diaphragmatic hiatus where the herniated stomach folds over the crura; mechanical trauma from diaphragmatic movement causes ischemic ulcers → chronic blood loss → iron deficiency anemia

Gastric volvulus: in paraesophageal hernias, the stomach can rotate around its longitudinal axis (organoaxial — 2/3 of cases) or around the gastrohepatic omentum (mesenteroaxial — 1/3); rotation >180° causes closed-loop obstruction and ischemia

Organoaxial volvulus: stomach rotates along the axis connecting GEJ and pylorus; greater curvature rotates upward → fundus and body become twisted → closed-loop obstruction → venous congestion → arterial ischemia → perforation if untreated

Respiratory compromise: large paraesophageal hernia compresses the left lower lobe → atelectasis, dyspnea, postprandial hypoxia; in giant hernias, the entire left hemithorax may be occupied by stomach → restrictive lung physiology

Esophageal shortening: chronic inflammation from reflux causes fibrosis and longitudinal muscle contraction → the esophagus shortens → the GEJ is pulled up → perpetuates the hernia and makes surgical reduction more difficult

Obesity-related pathogenesis: increased intra-abdominal pressure (25-30 mmHg in severe obesity vs 5-7 mmHg normal) → constant mechanical stress on the hiatus → progressive widening → higher recurrence after surgical repair

Rising GERD prevalence in East Africa: dietary changes (high-fat, refined foods), rising obesity rates (especially urban Kenyan women), and reduced H. pylori prevalence (which may have had a protective effect against GERD through inducing atrophic gastritis)
Hiatal hernia in COPD/chronic cough: chronic cough generates intra-thoracic pressure changes and diaphragmatic strain → progressive weakening of hiatal support → increased hernia size over time

Iron deficiency anemia: 15-30% of patients with large paraesophageal hernias have iron deficiency anemia from Cameron ulcers; the ulcers are often endoscopically subtle (linear, at the hernia neck) and easily missed

East African surgical context: laparoscopic fundoplication is available in Nairobi and major referral centers but most district hospitals manage with PPI and observation; paraesophageal hernias present acutely with volvulus due to delayed diagnosis

landmarkTrials.population: 554 patients with chronic GERD randomized to laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication vs esomeprazole 20-40 mg daily
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication vs long-term esomeprazole therapy
landmarkTrials.outcome: 5-year remission: surgery 85% vs PPI 92% (by symptom assessment). Surgery superior for regurgitation and atypical symptoms. PPI excellent for heartburn control. Patient satisfaction similar.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: PPI therapy more accessible than surgery in Kenya; surgery reserved for PPI-refractory cases or paraesophageal hernias

landmarkTrials.population: 217 patients with GERD randomized to Nissen fundoplication vs esomeprazole 20-40 mg
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication vs esomeprazole for 12 months
landmarkTrials.outcome: GERD remission at 12 months: surgery 65% vs PPI 92%. Surgery significantly improved regurgitation (84% vs 15% complete relief). Both groups: improved quality of life. Surgery related to more dysphagia and bloating.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Confirms PPI efficacy for heartburn but surgery superior for regurgitation — relevant for Kenyan patients with volume reflux

landmarkTrials.trial: Paraesophageal Hernia Repair: Laparoscopic vs Open (2011, Annals of Surgery — Systematic Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 16 studies, 1834 patients undergoing paraesophageal hernia repair
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic vs open paraesophageal hernia repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Laparoscopic: shorter hospital stay (2.5 vs 7.5 days), fewer wound complications, comparable recurrence rates (15% vs 14%). Conversion rate 6%. Operative time longer (210 vs 175 min).
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Laparoscopic approach preferred in tertiary Kenyan centers; open repair remains standard at district level

landmarkTrials.trial: Crural Repair: Mesh vs Suture Alone (2016, JAMA — Multicenter RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 206 patients undergoing laparoscopic hiatal hernia repair
landmarkTrials.intervention: Crural repair with absorbable mesh (biosynthetic) vs suture alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: Hernia recurrence at 2 years: mesh 12% vs suture 16% (p=0.42). No significant difference. Mesh group: lower reoperation rate (1% vs 5%). No mesh-related erosions with absorbable mesh.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Absorbable mesh not widely available in Kenya; suture-only repair is acceptable standard

landmarkTrials.trial: Nissen vs Toupet Fundoplication (2010, Annals of Surgery — RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 200 patients with GERD randomized to Nissen (360°) vs Toupet (270°) fundoplication
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic Nissen vs Toupet fundoplication for GERD
landmarkTrials.outcome: 5-year follow-up: comparable reflux control (92% vs 90%). Toupet: lower dysphagia rate (5% vs 15%, p<0.01), less gas bloat. Nissen: slightly better regurgitation control. Toupet preferred for patients with preoperative dysphagia or weak esophageal motility.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Toupet preferred in Kenyan setting where esophageal manometry may not be available preoperatively to assess motility

landmarkTrials.trial: Short Gastric Vessel Division in Nissen (2001, Annals of Surgery — RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 102 patients randomized to Nissen with vs without division of short gastric vessels
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication with vs without short gastric vessel division
landmarkTrials.outcome: No difference in dysphagia, reflux control, or wrap tightness at 12 months. Division increases operative time and bleeding risk. Routine division not recommended.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Sparing short gastric vessels reduces operative time and cost — both important in Kenyan surgical practice

landmarkTrials.trial: Gastric Volvulus: Acute Management Outcomes (2013, World Journal of Surgery)
landmarkTrials.population: 137 patients with acute gastric volvulus — multicenter retrospective
landmarkTrials.intervention: Emergency surgery: reduction, gastropexy (± fundoplication, ± resection)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Overall mortality 12% (higher with delayed surgery >24h: 20% vs <6h: 5%). Resection required in 15% for gangrene. Laparoscopic approach feasible in hemodynamically stable patients.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Highlights importance of early surgical intervention for acute volvulus — relevant in Kenya where presentation may be delayed

landmarkTrials.trial: Endoscopic Therapies for GERD: Stretta vs TIF vs Sham (2021, Gastroenterology — Systematic Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 28 RCTs comparing endoscopic GERD therapies to sham or PPI
landmarkTrials.intervention: Stretta (radiofrequency), Transoral Incisionless Fundoplication (TIF), or sham procedure
landmarkTrials.outcome: TIF: improved GERD-HRQL and reduced PPI use at 6 months, but efficacy diminished by 3-5 years. Stretta: no significant benefit over sham. Both inferior to laparoscopic fundoplication.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Endoscopic GERD therapies not available in Kenya; fundoplication remains standard surgical option

evidenceTable.effect: Surgery: 85% clinical remission; PPI: 92% symptom control
evidenceTable.recommendation: Fundoplication for PPI-refractory GERD or paraesophageal hernia

evidenceTable.effect: Toupet: dysphagia 5% vs Nissen 15%; reflux control comparable (90% vs 92%)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Toupet preferred if preoperative dysphagia or poor motility

evidenceTable.effect: Mesh: 12% vs Suture: 16% (p=0.42) — no significant difference
evidenceTable.recommendation: Suture-only repair acceptable; mesh reserved for large defects (>5 cm)

evidenceTable.therapy: Laparoscopic vs Open paraesophageal hernia repair (Systematic Review)
evidenceTable.effect: Laparoscopic: 2.5 days, Open: 7.5 days; comparable recurrence ~15%
evidenceTable.recommendation: Laparoscopic approach preferred; open if laparoscopic expertise unavailable

evidenceTable.therapy: Elective vs Emergency repair of paraesophageal hernia
evidenceTable.recommendation: Elective repair for all symptomatic paraesophageal hernias

evidenceTable.therapy: Cameron ulcers: PPI + iron vs surgery
evidenceTable.population: Iron deficiency anemia from Cameron ulcers in paraesophageal hernia
evidenceTable.effect: PPI + iron: 65% normalization; hernia repair: 90% normalization (p<0.05)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Consider hernia repair for PPI-refractory Cameron ulcer anemia

evidenceTable.effect: Higher failure rate in obesity (RR 1.5-2.0); Roux-en-Y gastric bypass preferred for BMI >35 with GERD
evidenceTable.recommendation: Consider bariatric surgery if BMI >35 with GERD; higher recurrence after fundoplication in obesity

evidenceTable.therapy: PPI therapy for GERD from hiatal hernia
evidenceTable.effect: PPI: 80-90% healing of erosive esophagitis; superior to H2RA
evidenceTable.recommendation: PPI first-line medical therapy for GERD with hiatal hernia

evidenceTable.therapy: Short gastric vessel division in Nissen (RCT)
evidenceTable.effect: No difference at 12 months; division increases operative time
evidenceTable.recommendation: Routine division not necessary; mobilize fundus enough for tension-free wrap

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical suspicion — classic GERD symptoms (heartburn, regurgitation), retrosternal chest pain, postprandial dyspnea, chronic cough, hoarseness, iron deficiency anemia (Cameron ulcers)

Step 2: Barium swallow (upper GI series) — first-line imaging for diagnosis and classification (Type I-IV). Assess GEJ position relative to diaphragm, hernia size, organoaxial rotation, and any reflux on fluoroscopy

Step 3: Upper GI endoscopy — essential to assess esophagitis (LA classification), Barrett's esophagus, Cameron ulcers, stricture, and exclude malignancy. Document hernia size and type retroflexed view
Step 4: ECG and cardiac workup in patients with chest pain — rule out cardiac ischemia before attributing chest pain to hiatal hernia (especially in older patients with cardiac risk factors)
Step 5: CT Chest/Abdomen — for large/complicated paraesophageal hernias (Type III-IV). Assess hernia contents (stomach alone vs colon/omentum/spleen), organoaxial volvulus, and pulmonary compromise

Step 6: Esophageal manometry — pre-operative assessment before fundoplication. Rules out achalasia and severe motility disorders (contraindication to Nissen fundoplication). Assess LES pressure and esophageal peristalsis
Step 7: 24-hour pH monitoring — if diagnosis of GERD remains uncertain after endoscopy and symptoms; DeMeester score >14.7 confirms pathologic reflux; correlates reflux episodes with symptoms

Step 8: Multidisciplinary assessment — surgeon, gastroenterologist, and if surgical candidate: anesthesiologist (risk of aspiration), cardiopulmonary assessment for large hernias causing respiratory compromise
Step 9: Treat underlying contributors — weight loss (target BMI <30), smoking cessation, COPD optimization, constipation management; these reduce recurrence after surgical repair

Step 10: Shared decision making — asymptomatic Type I: observation. Symptomatic Type I: PPI vs fundoplication. Any symptomatic Type II-IV: elective repair. Acute volvulus/strangulation: emergency surgery

Kenya adaptation: Barium swallow is widely available and cost-effective; endoscopy available at provincial/tertiary centers; manometry and pH monitoring limited to Nairobi; clinical diagnosis with PPI trial is pragmatic at district level
managementPearls: Not all hiatal hernias need surgery — asymptomatic Type I (sliding) hernias can be managed with PPI and lifestyle modification; only 15-20% of patients with GERD will eventually require fundoplication
Symptomatic paraesophageal hernias (Types II-IV) should be repaired electively — the elective mortality is 1-2% vs emergency mortality of 12-20% when volvulus or strangulation occurs

Complete mediastinal hernia sac excision is the most critical step in preventing recurrence — a residual sac acts as a 'pseudosac' that can re-herniate; dissect sac off pericardium, pleura, and aorta using sharp dissection and gentle traction

A tension-free crural repair is essential: if the hiatus defect is >5 cm, consider relaxing incisions (right crus) or mesh reinforcement to reduce recurrence; simple suture repair alone has 15-20% recurrence even in expert hands

A 56-60 Fr bougie must be passed before completing the Nissen wrap to prevent a too-tight wrap causing dysphagia; the wrap should be 'floppy' — the surgeon should be able to pass a dissector between the wrap and the bougie

Short esophagus is a trap: if <3 cm of intra-abdominal esophagus cannot be achieved after full mediastinal mobilization, perform a Collis gastroplasty (wedge fundectomy to create a neoesophagus) rather than leaving tension on the repair

Nissen (360°) fundoplication provides better reflux control; Toupet (270°) causes less dysphagia and gas bloat — choose Toupet when preoperative manometry shows weak peristalsis (<50% effective swallows) or amplitude <30 mmHg

In East Africa, where laparoscopic equipment and mesh may be limited, open transabdominal hiatal hernia repair with suture cruroplasty and Nissen fundoplication is a safe and effective alternative with comparable long-term outcomes

Postoperative dysphagia is common in the first 2-4 weeks due to edema — reassure patients, advance diet slowly, and avoid unnecessary investigations; if dysphagia persists >3 months, investigate with barium swallow and endoscopy

Gas bloat syndrome (inability to belch, abdominal distension) affects 10-15% of Nissen patients — treat with dietary modification (avoid carbonated drinks, eat slowly), simethicone, and time; severe cases may require conversion to Toupet

Cameron ulcers should be suspected in any elderly patient with unexplained iron deficiency anemia and a large paraesophageal hernia — the ulcers are often at the hernia neck and easily missed on endoscopy; look carefully in the retroflexed view

Recurrence after paraesophageal hernia repair is 10-30% at 5 years — most are radiographic recurrences without symptoms; only 5-10% require reoperation. Risk factors: obesity, chronic cough, heavy lifting, large initial defect
operativeNuances: Patient positioning: split-leg supine with steep reverse Trendelenburg (30°) allows gravity to retract the transverse colon and small bowel away from the hiatus; surgeon stands between the patient's legs for optimal ergonomic access

Liver retraction is critical: use a flexible liver retractor (Nathanson or snake retractor) placed through a subxiphoid port to elevate the left lobe; inadequate retraction of the hypertrophied left lobe in obese patients is a common cause of difficult exposure

Hiatal dissection: start at the right crus — identify the right crus at its insertion on the vertebral column, develop the plane between the crus and the esophagus, then continue anteriorly across the vagus nerves to the left crus

Identify and preserve both vagus nerves: the anterior vagus trunk runs on the anterior esophageal surface; the posterior vagus runs separately often adherent to the right crus; vagal injury causes gastroparesis and postoperative bloating

Mediastinal sac dissection: use a 30° laparoscope for better visualization; enter the 'yellow' plane between the hernia sac and the mediastinal structures; avoid entry into the pleura (transparent membrane posteriorly) — if pneumothorax occurs, close with a stitch on the diaphragm or leave a small tube

Crural closure: begin with the posterior crura using 0-Ethibond or 0-Surgilon sutures on a SH needle; place sutures 1 cm apart starting at the base of the crura; tie gently — excessive tension on the crural sutures causes tearing and recurrence

If the hiatus is very wide and tension-free closure is not possible with 2-3 posterior sutures alone, consider a relaxing incision on the right crus (incising the right crus perpendicular to its long axis) or use biosynthetic mesh reinforcement

Fundoplication creation: after passing the 56 Fr bougie, bring the fundus behind the esophagus (the 'window' is created during hiatal dissection); the fundus should lie freely without torsion — the 'shoe-shine' maneuver confirms proper positioning

The wrap should be 2-2.5 cm long and secured with three non-absorbable sutures (0-silk or 0-Ethibond) that include a bite of the anterior esophageal wall (to prevent slippage); place the most cephalad suture first to set the wrap position

In resource-limited Kenyan settings, reusable laparoscopic instruments (5 mm, 33 cm length) and a 0° laparoscope work adequately for most fundoplications; the 30° laparoscope is preferred for mediastinal dissection but not essential

For acute gastric volvulus: if the stomach is viable, perform laparoscopic or open detorsion, gastropexy (PEG or suture fixation to anterior abdominal wall), and hiatal hernia repair; if gangrenous, perform partial gastrectomy

prognosis.type1SlidingHernia: Excellent prognosis with PPI therapy (90%+ symptom control); surgical fundoplication provides 85-90% satisfaction at 5-10 years with 5-10% recurrence
prognosis.types2_4ParaesophagealElective: Elective repair: 90%+ good to excellent outcomes at 5 years; 1-2% mortality; 10-15% radiographic recurrence; 5-10% reoperation rate
prognosis.types2_4ParaesophagealEmergency: Emergency repair for volvulus/strangulation: 12-20% mortality; higher complication rate (30-50%); 15% require gastric resection for gangrene
prognosis.cameronUlcerAnemia: Hernia repair resolves anemia in 85-90% of patients; PPI + iron therapy effective in 65%
prognosticFactors: Hernia type and size — Type I has best prognosis; giant Type IV hernias have highest recurrence risk
Obesity (BMI >30) — increases recurrence 1.5-2x; weight loss before repair improves outcomes
Preoperative esophageal motility — poor motility predicts postoperative dysphagia if Nissen performed
Symptom duration — longer duration correlates with esophageal shortening and more difficult repair
Surgeon experience — high-volume centers have 30-50% lower recurrence rates (learning curve ~50 cases)
Compliance with postoperative restrictions — early heavy lifting and failure to modify lifestyle predict recurrence
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Rising obesity rates in urban Kenya will increase hiatal hernia incidence; PPI therapy is widely available (omeprazole on essential medicines list); laparoscopic fundoplication limited to 4-5 centers nationally; most paraesophageal hernias present emergently with volvulus due to delayed diagnosis and limited surgical access

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Incarcerated/strangulated/volvulus -> SEVERE; sliding hernia without complication -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Do you have heartburn (a burning sensation rising from your stomach to your throat)?
Do you taste food or sour liquid in your mouth after eating?
Do you have difficulty swallowing food?
Do you have chest pain behind your breastbone?
Is the chest pain worse after eating or when lying down?
Do you feel short of breath after meals?
Do you feel full or bloated after eating only a small amount?
Have you been diagnosed with anemia or low blood?
Do you take medication for heartburn (omeprazole, antacids)?
Are your symptoms worse when you bend forward or lie flat?
Do you have a chronic cough, especially at night?
Do you have asthma or wheezing that is worse after meals?
Are you overweight or have you gained weight recently?
Do you do heavy lifting or manual farm work?
Have you ever had an ECG or heart check? Was it normal?
Do you eat large meals or eat close to bedtime?
Do you have chronic constipation and strain on the toilet?
Have you had previous surgery on your stomach or diaphragm?
Do you feel a gurgling sensation in your chest after eating?
Do you wake up with a bitter taste in your mouth or coughing at night?
Have you been told you have asthma or bronchitis that does not respond to inhalers?
Do you have heartburn that wakes you from sleep?
Have you lost your voice or developed hoarseness, especially in the morning?
Do you have a feeling of a lump in your throat (globus sensation)?
Do you avoid certain foods because they cause chest discomfort?
Have you ever vomited blood or had black stools?
Do you have chronic constipation and strain when passing stool?
Do you smoke cigarettes or use other tobacco products?
Do you have COPD, chronic bronchitis, or a chronic cough?
Have you had previous surgery on your stomach or esophagus?
Do you have Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos, or joint hypermobility?
Have you had an episode where you suddenly felt severe chest pain with retching but could not vomit? (sign of volvulus)
Are you pregnant or have you been pregnant multiple times?
Do you take steroids, immunosuppressants, or NSAIDs regularly?
Have you had an ECG or cardiac workup done for your chest pain?
Do you have difficulty belching or passing gas after meals?
Do you have a hiatus hernia or GERD that runs in your family?

examination_findings.finding: Epigastric tenderness on deep palpation
Pallor from iron deficiency anemia
Decreased breath sounds at left lung base from herniated stomach
Tympanic percussion over left chest (stomach in chest)
Bowel sounds heard over left chest (pathognomonic for paraesophageal)
Signs of strangulation — severe epigastric/chest tenderness, guarding
Distended upper abdomen with scaphoid lower abdomen
Signs of dehydration from chronic vomiting
Fever — if strangulation or perforation
Tachycardia from pain, dehydration, or strangulation
Hypotension if strangulation or bleeding
Visible peristalsis in upper abdomen
Obesity — common associated finding
Bowel sounds audible on auscultation over the left chest (pathognomonic for paraesophageal hernia)
Tympanic percussion over left lower chest (Stomach replaces lung resonance)
Decreased breath sounds at left base from compression by herniated stomach
Tracheal deviation to the right (from large left-sided mediastinal mass effect)
Visible epigastric fullness or 'doughy' sensation on deep epigastric palpation
Pallor of conjunctiva, palmar creases, and nail beds from iron deficiency anemia
Angular stomatitis and koilonychia (spoon nails) from chronic iron deficiency
Signs of acute volvulus: severe epigastric tenderness, board-like rigidity, distended upper abdomen with scaphoid lower abdomen
Tachycardia and hypotension if strangulation or perforation has occurred
Retching (non-productive vomiting) — Borchardt triad: epigastric pain, retching, inability to vomit
Signs of dehydration from chronic vomiting or poor intake
Fever and peritonism if perforation from volvulus
Globus sensation on palpation of the neck (thyroid should be examined to exclude goiter causing swallowing symptoms)
Dental enamel erosion from chronic GERD (posterior teeth involved)
Halitosis (foul breath) from regurgitation and reflux
Finger clubbing (rare — associated with chronic pulmonary aspiration from GERD)

bedside_scores.name: GERD Symptom Score (GerdQ)
bedside_scores.interpretation: GerdQ ≥8: high probability GERD; response to PPI supports diagnosis

riskFactors.note: Increased intra-abdominal pressure pushes GEJ through hiatus
Weakening of phrenoesophageal ligament with age
COPD, TB sequelae, smoking — all cause chronic cough
Occupational risk in farming and manual labor

subtypes.name: Type I (Sliding) Hiatal Hernia
subtypes.management: Lifestyle modification (weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, avoid large meals). PPI therapy. Laparoscopic fundoplication (Nissen or Toupet) if refractory to medical therapy or if patient prefers surgery.

subtypes.name: Types II-IV (Paraesophageal) Hiatal Hernia
subtypes.management: Surgical repair recommended for all symptomatic paraesophageal hernias and asymptomatic ones with significant herniation. Laparoscopic hernia reduction, sac excision, crural repair with mesh, and fundoplication. Emergency surgery if acute gastric volvulus — laparotomy, derotation, gastropexy ± resection if gangrene.

mimics.whyMimic: Retrosternal chest pain can mimic cardiac angina
Dysphagia and chest pain can mimic hiatal hernia
Chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath
Shortness of breath, cough, wheezing, chest tightness
Dysphagia, weight loss, chest pain, regurgitation
Chest pain, dyspnea, cough, retrosternal discomfort
Anterior chest pain worse with movement, may mimic reflux chest pain
Postprandial fullness, vomiting, weight loss, epigastric distension
Sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, tachycardia

bedside.name: ECG
bedside.indication: Rule out cardiac cause of chest pain

laboratory.name: Iron studies (Ferritin, TIBC, Iron)
laboratory.indication: Iron deficiency from chronic blood loss

laboratory.name: H. pylori testing
laboratory.indication: Rule out H. pylori if dyspepsia

imaging.name: Barium Swallow
imaging.indication: Best for demonstrating hernia anatomy, type, and GEJ position

imaging.name: Upper GI Endoscopy (EGD)
imaging.indication: Assess for esophagitis, Cameron ulcers, Barrett's, stricture

imaging.name: CT Chest/Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Assess for complications, volvulus, type IV hernia contents

imaging.name: Esophageal Manometry
imaging.indication: Pre-operative assessment of esophageal motility before fundoplication

imaging.name: 24-hour pH monitoring
imaging.indication: If diagnosis of GERD uncertain; pre-op evaluation

management.protocolReference: SAGES Guidelines for Hiatal Hernia Management (2023)

resuscitation: NG tube — attempt placement cautiously; if resistance, stop (may be in hernia sac)
Emergency assessment for strangulation/volvulus: urgent CT
conservative: Lifestyle modification: weight loss, head-of-bed elevation (15-30 cm)
Avoid: large meals, eating within 3 hours of bedtime, bending after meals
PPI therapy: Omeprazole 20-40 mg daily or Pantoprazole 40 mg daily
Antacids or alginates PRN for breakthrough symptoms

surgical.description: Laparoscopic Fundoplication (Nissen or Toupet) ± Crural Repair
surgical.indication: Type I: PPI-refractory GERD. Types II-IV: all symptomatic paraesophageal hernias
surgical.step4: Crural dissection — identify both crura, mobilize esophagus
surgical.step5: Crural closure — interrupted 0-Ethibond sutures ± mesh reinforcement
surgical.step7: Stitch fundoplication to crura to prevent recurrence
surgical.step8: Check wrap tension — should admit a 56 Fr bougie

nonOperative: PPI therapy — lifelong for patients unfit for surgery
Endoscopic procedures: Stretta (radiofrequency), transoral incisionless fundoplication (TIF)
Observation for asymptomatic Type I hernias

general: Water-soluble contrast swallow on postop day 1 (optional routine)
Clear fluids day 1, soft diet day 2-3
diet_advancement: Day 1: Clear fluids — water, tea, clear soup
Day 2-3: Soft blenderized diet — porridge (uji), yoghurt, smoothies
Day 3-7: Soft diet — mashed potatoes, eggs, soft fish, well-cooked vegetables
Week 2+: Transition to regular diet — chew well, small portions
Lifelong: small frequent meals, avoid gas-forming foods, carbonated drinks
Never: eat large meals, eat quickly, or lie flat after eating
discharge_criteria: Tolerating soft diet without dysphagia or vomiting
No evidence of wrap migration or leak on imaging

pathways.criteria: Reflux symptoms, small sliding hernia, no complications
pathways.treatment: PPI therapy. Lifestyle modification. Laparoscopic fundoplication for PPI-refractory GERD.

pathways.criteria: Symptomatic paraesophageal hernia, incarceration, volvulus, Cameron ulcers with anemia
pathways.treatment: Emergency surgery if acute (volvulus, strangulation). Elective laparoscopic repair: hernia reduction, sac excision, crural repair, fundoplication.

operative_templates.procedure: Laparoscopic Nissen Fundoplication with Crural Repair
operative_templates.indication: Hiatal hernia with GERD refractory to medical therapy; paraesophageal hernia
steps: Supine, split-leg, reverse Trendelenburg, surgeon between legs
5-port setup: umbilical camera, bilateral subcostal, left flank, subxiphoid
Retract left lobe of liver using liver retractor
Divide gastrohepatic ligament, identify right crus
Reduce hernia contents from mediastinum using gentle traction
Excise hernia sac completely from mediastinum
Mobilize distal esophagus — achieve 3 cm intra-abdominal length
Divide short gastric vessels (2-3 vessels) to mobilize gastric fundus
Three gastro-gastric sutures to create 2 cm wrap
Secure wrap to crura with 1-2 sutures

watchFor: absent breath sounds on one side
barium swallow shows wrap position abnormal

dischargeCriteria: No chest pain or shortness of breath
Able to belch (to confirm wrap not too tight)
Patient educated on dietary restrictions and activity limitations

patient_education.explanation: A hiatal hernia is when part of your stomach pushes up through the diaphragm into your chest. The diaphragm is the muscle that separates your chest from your belly and helps keep stomach acid down. When it weakens, acid can come up (GERD). Paraesophageal hernias are more serious because the stomach can twist (volvulus) or lose its blood supply.
patient_education.warningSigns: Go to the emergency department immediately if: you have severe chest or upper abdominal pain with retching but cannot vomit, you cannot pass a drinking tube or swallow, you vomit blood, you have high fever with severe pain, or you have sudden shortness of breath.
patient_education.activity: After surgery: walk daily. Avoid heavy lifting (>5 kg) for 6-8 weeks. Avoid straining on the toilet — use stool softeners if needed. Sleep with head elevated. Do not bend forward from the waist for 4 weeks.
patient_education.diet: Eat small, frequent meals — do not overfill the stomach. Eat slowly and chew well. Do not lie down for at least 2 hours after eating. Avoid: carbonated drinks (soda, beer), very hot or very cold drinks, fatty and fried foods, coffee, alcohol. Good foods: porridge (uji), rice, bananas, boiled vegetables, lean meats, fish, yoghurt.
patient_education.medication: Take omeprazole or pantoprazole as prescribed. After fundoplication, you may need PPI for 4 weeks then stop. Do not take NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) regularly — they can cause ulcers. Use paracetamol for pain.
patient_education.followUp: 2 weeks: clinic review and wound check. 6 weeks: assess symptoms, diet advancement. 3 months: barium swallow if symptoms persist. Annual follow-up for surveillance.

before_discharge: Dietary teaching: small frequent meals, avoid carbonated drinks

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic review — wound check, symptom assessment
Diet advancement to regular, clinical assessment
Clinical review — assess for recurrence of reflux or dysphagia
Annual follow-up if asymptomatic; endoscopy if symptoms return

when_to_return: Inability to swallow liquids or saliva
Shortness of breath or severe bloating
